description_long: This structure is a ruined Greco-Roman style temple with a rectangular stone base and a series of tall, fluted columns supporting a partially collapsed entablature and pediment. The roof is mostly missing, with only fragments of the triangular pediment and some beams remaining. The temple is overgrown with green vines and scattered foliage, which drape over the columns and fallen architectural elements. Several columns are broken or toppled, lying across the entrance and sides, while others remain upright but damaged. The stonework features simple geometric patterns, particularly a Greek key motif along the entablature. The interior is open and accessible, with debris and plant growth visible throughout.
description: A ruined Greco-Roman stone temple with broken columns, a partially collapsed roof, and overgrown vines. The structure is open and accessible, with scattered debris and classical geometric patterns.
description_view_0: Side view showing upright and fallen columns, extensive vine growth, and a mostly open interior.
description_view_1: Frontal view highlighting the main entrance with a triangular pediment, broken columns, and vines draping over the entryway.
description_view_2: Opposite side view with more visible fallen columns and dense foliage, revealing the open structure and interior debris.
description_view_3: Rear view focusing on the back pediment, upright columns, and vines climbing the structure.
attribute_ornamentation: sparse geometric patterns, Greek key motif, minimal additional decoration, natural overgrowth of vines
attribute_entrance_design: columned portico with a triangular pediment, flanked by broken and upright columns, overgrown with vines and debris at the base
